---
title: "Tidyr tutorial"
output:
  learnr::tutorial:
    progressive: true
    allow_skip: true
    css: css/style.scss
runtime: shiny_prerendered
favicon: images/NEOF_favicon.png
description: >
  Tutorial covering tidyr package 
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(learnr)
learnr::tutorial_options(exercise.completion = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(lterdatasampler)
library(mgrtibbles)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

## Tidyr

<center>![](./images/tidyr.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

This tutorial covers the `tidyr` package.
Its purposes is to create tidy data which has three main features:

- Each variable is a column; each column is a variable
- Each observation is a row; each row is an observation
- Each value is a cell; each cell is a single value

Tidy data is particularly important for using `ggplot2` for making data visualisations.

This tutorial will have three main sections:

- __Pivoting:__ Changing the structure of a __tibble__ to long or wide
  - `ggplot2` requires data/tibbles to be long
- __Character vectors:__ Separating and uniting character columns
- __Missing values:__ Removing or replacing NAs in tibbles.

[Tidyverse Tidyr page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/tidyr.html)

## Long versus wide

__Wide__ and __long__ data/tables contain the same information but are structured differently.

### Wide

__Wide__ data is great for humans to read but is not optimal for manipulation and analysis with `tidyverse` packages.

__Wide__ data contains multiple columns of the same metric.
It is what most people are familiar with when looking at data in a table.

Below is an example of a __wide tibble__.
It shows the captured production of fisheries in metric tons. 

```{r wide-tibble}
#Countries to filter
countries <- c("Afghanistan", "Albania", "Algeria", "Andorra")
fisheries_wide_tbl |>
  dplyr::filter(Entity %in% countries)
```

The __wide tibble__ is quite easy to read and you can quickly find the value of the entity and year you are interested in visually.
The features of this wide __tibble__ include:

- One row per Entity
- One column per Year
- The metric ton values are spread throughout columns and rows.

### Long

__Long__ data is not very human readable, being very long/tall, but is required for `tidyverse` packages and is easier to manipulate.

__Long__ data has each observation/value is in its own row.
There will be multiple columns of metadata but only one row of the values of interest.

Below is an example of a __long tibble__ containing the same data as the __wide tibble__ above:

```{r long-tibble}
#Countries to filter
countries <- c("Afghanistan", "Albania", "Algeria", "Andorra")
fisheries_long_tbl |>
  dplyr::filter(Entity %in% countries)
```

The __long tibble__ is harder to read but much better suited for `tidyverse`.
In fact, the previous tutorials have all been using __long tibbles__.

Features of the __long tibble__ include:

- Each observation (Metric_tons) is in its own row
- There are multiple rows per Entity
- The Year values are in their own column rather than being column names

Due to these differences the __long tibble__ has more rows (226 vs 4) whilst the __wide tibble__ has more columns (60 vs 3 ).
In other words, the __long tibble__ is longer and the __wide tibble__ is wider.

## pivot_longer()

<center>![](./images/tidyr_pivot_longer.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

The `pivot_longer()` function is a relatively straightforward function for longifying a tibble.

### Pivot longer exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenges.

[Tidyr pivot_longer() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/pivot_longer.html)

### Challenge 1

Longify the tibble `bat_roost_wide_tbl` so you have the following columns:

- __Area__
- __Species:__ The Species names, currently set as column names
- __MeanMaxCountInAnyYear:__ The Mean max count in any year, currently set as values

```{r longer-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
bat_roost_wide_tbl
```

```{r longer-1-exercise-solution}
bat_roost_wide_tbl |>
  #Longify tibble
  tidyr::pivot_longer(!Area, names_to="Species", values_to="MeanMaxCountInAnyYear")
```

### Challenge 2

Create the below long tibble with the wide tibble, `living_planet_wide_tbl`.

```{r living-long-tbl}
living_planet_wide_tbl |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(!Region, names_to="Year", values_to="AverageIndex")
```

```{r longer-2-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
living_planet_wide_tbl
```

```{r longer-2-exercise-solution}
living_planet_wide_tbl |>
  #Longify tibble
  tidyr::pivot_longer("1970":"2018", names_to="Year", values_to="AverageIndex")
```

Brilliant!
The next function is `pivot_wider()`.

## pivot_wider()

<center>![](./images/tidyr_pivot_wider.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

The `pivot_wider()` function is a relatively straightforward function for widening a tibble.

### Pivot longer exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenge.

[Tidyr pivot_wider() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/pivot_wider.html)

### Challenge 1

__Widen__ the `living_planet_tbl` tibble so it contains the `Average Index` values across the years.
You will need to :

- Remove the columns `Upper Index` and `Lower Index` before widening
  - The function `dplyr::select()` can be used
- Widen the tibble
  - Take the names from the `Year` column
  - Take the values from the `Average Index` column

- __Note:__ Column/variable names with spaces need to be quoted with backticks (`` `Average Index` ``.

```{r wider-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
living_planet_tbl
```

```{r wider-1-exercise-solution}
living_planet_tbl |>
  #Select wanted columns
  dplyr::select(Region:`Average Index`) |>
  #Widen tibble
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from=Year, values_from=`Average Index`)
```

Excellent!
Its time to look at sperating and uniting character vector columns.

## Character vectors

Next we'll see how we can seperate or combine character vector columns with:

- `separate_wider_delim()`: Splits a string column into multiple columns by a delimiter.
- `unite()`: Combines/unites multiple string columns into one column.

## seperate_wider()
<center>![](./images/tidyr_separate_wider.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

The `separate_wider_delim()` function can split a character column into multiple columns.

### Separate exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenge.

[Tidyr separate_wider_delim() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/separate_wider.html)

### Challenge 1

Separate the column `Area` of the `bat_roost_wide_tbl` to the following columns: `County`, `Region`, and `Country`.
The delimiter is `;`.

```{r separate-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
bat_roost_wide_tbl
```

```{r separate-1-exercise-solution}
bat_roost_wide_tbl |>
  tidyr::separate_wider_delim(Area, delim=";",
    names = c("County","Region","Country"))
```

Super!
We will look at `separate_wider()`'s opposite: `unite()`.

## unite()
<center>![](./images/tidyr_unite.png){style="width:150px"}</center>


The `unite()` function can combine multiple character columns into one.

### Unite exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenges.

[Tidyr unite() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/unite.html)

### Challenge 1

With the `amphibian_div_tbl` unite the below columns in the specified order to create the column `Lineage`.
Use the separator `;` and do not retain the original columns.

1. `Order`
2. `Family`
3. `Genus`
4. `Species`

```{r unite-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
amphibian_div_tbl
```

```{r unite-1-exercise-solution}
amphibian_div_tbl |>
  unite("Lineage", c("Order","Family","Genus","Species"),
    sep=";")
```

### Challenge 2

With the `mushroom_tbl` unite the below columns in the specified order to create the column `cap_shape_surface_color`.
Use the separator `_` and retain the original columns.

1. `cap_shape`
2. `cap_surface`
3. `cap_color`

```{r unite-2-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
mushroom_tbl
```

```{r unite-2-exercise-solution}
mushroom_tbl |>
  unite(cap_shape_surface_color,
    c("cap_shape","cap_surface","cap_color"),
    sep = "_", remove=FALSE)
```

Fantastic!
Onto the final section: dropping and replacing `NAs`.

## Missing data

Many datasets will have missing data/values in the form of `NA`s.
For many things these can be left alone.
However, some calculations, such as mean, will result in a value of `NA` if an `NA` was in the data, not ideal.

there are two main functions that can be used to deal with `NA`s:

- `drop_na()`: Remove rows with NAs.
- `replace_na`()`: Replace NAs.

## drop_na()
<center>![](./images/tidyr_drop_na.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

The `drop_na()` function can remove rows containing `NA`s.

### Drop NA exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenges.

[Tidyr drop_na() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/drop_na.html)

### Challenge 1

Drop/remove all rows with an `NA`.

```{r drop-na-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
amphibian_div_tbl
```

```{r drop-na-1-exercise-solution}
amphibian_div_tbl |>
  tidyr::drop_na()
```

### Challenge 2

Drop/remove rows which have `NA`s in any of the columns from `Age_at_maturity_min_y` to `Offspring_size_max_mm`.

```{r drop-na-2-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
amphibian_div_tbl
```

```{r drop-na-2-exercise-solution}
amphibian_div_tbl |>
  tidyr::drop_na(Age_at_maturity_min_y:Offspring_size_max_mm)
```

Great!
Instead of dropping `NA`s you can replace them.

## replace_na()
<center>![](./images/tidyr_replace_na.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

The `replace_na()` function can replace `NA` values in columns.

### Replace NA exercise

Read through the below link and use it as a guide to complete the challenge.

[Tidyr replace_na() page](https://neof-workshops.github.io/Tidyverse/tidyr/replace_na.html)

### Challenge 1

Replace `NA`s in the column `non_dreaming` of the tibble `mammal_sleep_tbl` with `0`s.

```{r replace-na-1-exercise, exercise=TRUE, exercise.eval=FALSE}
mammal_sleep_tbl
```

```{r replace-na-1-exercise-solution}
mammal_sleep_tbl |>
  tidyr::replace_na(list(non_dreaming=0))
```

Splendid!

## Finish
<center>![](./images/finish_flag.png){style="width:150px"}</center>

Great effort on completing the `tidyr` tutorial.
You've completed challenges on pivotting tibbles, separating and uniting character columns, and dealing with `NA`s.

The next tutorial is the one I conider the most fun: cretaing plots with `ggplot2`.
